emergent_themes.description: Kitchen overcrowding in shared student housing creates stress that drives delivery ordering as an escape mechanism
Delivery framed as something that must be 'earned' through exercise, work, or achievement rather than everyday convenience
Strategic switching between all delivery platforms to maximize promotional value rather than platform loyalty
Poor awareness of student-specific subscription benefits despite being target demographic
Claimed delivery frequency significantly higher than actual behavior revealed in diary data

coding_summary: Josh represents a highly structured, budget-conscious student whose delivery behavior is driven more by social friction and reward psychology than convenience. Key findings: 1) Kitchen overcrowding in his 8-person house creates stress that drives takeaway ordering as an escape, 2) He frames delivery as an 'earned reward' rather than routine convenience, 3) He strategically arbitrages deals across all three platforms rather than showing loyalty, 4) Despite being target demographic, he was unaware of student subscription benefits, 5) His diary revealed zero delivery orders across 7 days, contradicting his interview claim of ordering 'once a week', suggesting actual frequency is much lower than stated.

coverage_gaps.reason: Josh provided limited insight into how value perception might change post-graduation beyond basic income effects
Josh did not express any stigma around ordering behavior, suggesting this may not be a concern for him
